speaker: interviewer
text: January 17, 1996, Molly Lewis, maiden name Kloc, Sheila Rodin-Novak, Chicago, Illinois, USA. And the interview will be conducted in English. My name is Sheila Rodin-Novak. Today is January 17th, 1996. I'm interviewing Molly Lewis in Chicago, Illinois. Molly, we want to thank you very much for letting us come here today and interview you. Would you please tell us what your name is and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my name is Molly Lewis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Molly is M-O-L-L-Y, and Lewis, L-E-W-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malka Kloc.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Malka is M-A-L-K-A, and Kloc, K-L-O-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and were you ever known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and then it was Lewis, right? The other name you were known by.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other name is Cmielewski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cmielewski. And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age is what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 50-- 57-- excuse me, seven-- [LAUGHS] Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us the city and the country of your birth and spell that for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the city is Horochow, H-O-R-O-C-H-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the country is Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us a little bit about what your life was like as a child growing up in Horochow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I include my parents' name, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I? I am the daughter of Rachel and Gershon Klotz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was raised in faith of believing in God, believing in good humanity, doing pioneer work, helping the others, and getting a good education.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. He had a few different times in his-- in-- difficult times, so therefore he had changed the business. He had once a clothing business, men's clothing, for a few years. Then due to a partner, he gave it up. And then he went, and it was like a [POLISH], we used to call it in Poland. It's many things. It was a grocery. It was dishes. It was lamps, fixtures, was even herring, everything at the big store. That time, when I became the age of six, we had a big fire. And what there were they couldn't throw out and didn't got broken outside, got inside burnt. And my mother got very, very hurt about that. In Poland, we didn't have no insurance. And-- although we had-- we had a cow, I remember. We had two horses. Because my-- then my father changed to burning wood. He got in partnership with a very rich family. The other one supplied the money, and my husband gave his ability--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, I'm sorry-- his business ability. And he bought like in the woods, wood, and came very-- he hired many wagons with horses, very-- you know the drivers-- and they came there and they picked up woods, which was already taking an order who ordered when they were [INAUDIBLE] to get to the wood, you know, to everybody. I started, since then, to help out my father and this business. How did I do it? He left me names who ordered and gave them a deposit. Whenever-- he used to be completely in the back of all the wagons, probably by a reason, OK. So he knew that he may depend on me, although I was a very young girl-- maybe I was in the age of 10. I don't know-- more or less. And when they came and to each wagon had consisted of a certain amount of-- kilos or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and I looked up the that and that what my husband-- my father left for me that-- what shall I say? You know, they knew I-- to whom, you know, the orders, the orders, the home. And I was-- I've done my job. I went really-- I sat down on the-- on the wagon and showed the drivers how to drive into that and that family, all around. And since then, I felt my father-- [INAUDIBLE] used to say this-- when I will grow up, I will become-- I will be a good business woman. [LAUGHS] And how many sisters and brothers did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had only one sister, her name was Pearl, and one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Israel. Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a child were you? Did you get into mischief? Did you-- were you a good girl? Did you play around a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, then we were in Israel, I found out if a friend which was a friend of my mother. And I remember her when she used to come to our house. And they were actually very rich people. But they liked each other and then they got into our family by marriage. Her brother married my-- my mother's first cousin. And they were very rich people, as I said before. And she said to my husband, Malka-- Molly was known to everyone but a good daughter, a good girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things did you like to do as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked to draw. Being in school, in the public school, I was drawing flowers which was given to-- to our teachers at their birthdays. And I remember that once I got ill of measles and the picture was in my house, but I couldn't go to school. I had finished that sitting in bed with the colors. And the children knew that those are measles. They're not supposed to come to pick it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knocked at my mother's windows. And my mother asked me if she may handle that to them. And I said, boy, I'm happy that they came, and give them my love, and love to the teacher, through the window, knowing that if they will come in, they might will be catching the measles. And tell us a little bit about your religious background. How were you raised?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a religious fight as a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you Orthodox, observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an Orthodox man, and he taught me a lot, and how to observe the religious faith and to daven-- to pray, and to everything, although he was a good businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relationship with the non-Jewish community in Horochow? What-- did you have non-Jewish friends? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, being in school, in the public school, I had-- sure, I had good friends, girls. And happens I was a good student, sort of. I had a good memory. I remember once-- this was in a history-- the hour of history-- and, you know, the history teacher asked some questions and that time and that day she was requested. And she was sitting next to me. She always, to be. To make sure that I am not asleep, I shall tell her the answers. I was awake.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was standing, a very pretty girl, and-- and I told her there was a question of what year was a war and in 1914, and who was the king-- the king? And I told her [INAUDIBLE]. And was [INAUDIBLE]. I still remember that. But as she didn't read it, so she couldn't pronounce the [INAUDIBLE]. As she didn't read his name or she didn't pay attention when we had the lesson previously. So she sort of screwed up the name. And she over heared the-- the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I did-- I gave her the right, the correct date. Well, as she was standing and I was sitting so-- I was not supposed to do that. But in order to do a favor to the-- to my girlfriend, and the teacher shall not notify me. So my face and my mouth just hit at her back, the bottom part out of her back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she didn't answer correctly. And she knew that, that it was not correctly. So at the recess, she fought me very much. She started to hit me. You [POLISH]. This was-- she-- you specially gave me the wrong answers. What are you doing here? [SPEAKING POLISH] This is Polska. This is Poland. You go to Palestine. As you remember, my husband indicated something, I always repeat it. It was in my mind because I felt that I was very hurt. That day I came home from school very hurt and I cried. I said, you know, I think that [? Zoska ?] was-- [? Zoska ?] [INAUDIBLE], she was right by saying that. That the Jewish people should have a country. Why don't we work on that, to get the country? So my mother said, to get a country, we have to fight. We have to send sons. They shall be able to fight and willing to fight. That might will help. And she gave me a kiss on my head. She said, that's the head told me the right question. And the love to Israel was always in me. Did you have any other experiences of anti-Semitism when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, terrible experience since 1941?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before the war, before 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before 19-- as it-- we-- we heard many-- I was hurt once before the war. I walked into a bookstore to buy an atlas for geography to study. And there was a globe. And I walked in, there was already-- that was exactly the year when I-- when I graduated the public school. But in that-- at that time, I didn't have it. I couldn't afford it, probably. So I walked in to buy it. And those two men said, [SPEAKING POLISH]. We are not selling no nothing to the Jews. And I got a very scared. And I remember that was the second time when I was came home, and I was so terribly crying what will we-- if those two started, what will we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. OK, tell me a little bit about your education.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have seven grade public school. Anyone who finished a seven grade had their right, if you have money, to pay and to continue their education, to enter to a high school that was the second grade of high school finished. This is what kind of a good-- we-- we studied very good. We had a good education in the seventh grade. But sorry to say, and that time I couldn't continue my education because my mother [INAUDIBLE] was something like heart trouble she had. And my father once fell off the wagon and hurt his head. It was terribly bleeding. Which and maybe it took him to suffer for more than a year, and he passed away. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year-- what year did he die-- what year did he die in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that he passed away something like in '76.

speaker: interviewer
text: 70's? You mean '36?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- '36, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, continue-- about your education, we were talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And of course I used to go to the [? Tarbut ?] school, night, Hebrew school, evening classes after the public school. And then in 1937, I went [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] this is in-- before [INAUDIBLE] Zionist organization, they send me to help to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to build up a Batar [INAUDIBLE], which is like [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: There were a few when I came there. It's to learn how there were [INAUDIBLE]. You know? To learn us how hard we will have to work in Israel, be prepared. And they taught us a lot of things, how to work on the gardens, and yards, and many things. And so also the Hebrew education-- this was a Mizrahi. A Mizrahi-- young Mizrahi children. Was this kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- yeah, it's called-- it is like a kibbutz, yes. But the name was like-- it's not the name, but the general name was [INAUDIBLE]. What would-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK, that's all right, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you met Harry?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there-- no. From there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know the reason why, from Harry's kibbutz, they mailed the letter. And who introduced me, they shall ask for me, I don't know. I didn't know up until now. Maybe somebody who knew me, and he-- he was-- he was a [INAUDIBLE] or something, could be. Because I remember there used to be [INAUDIBLE] to interview, you know, good whatever-- with a good feeling to our country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden, this man [INAUDIBLE] came in. And he said Malka Kloc. Sorry, we love you here, but they want you to [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] which is near-- was like [INAUDIBLE]. Well, I don't know. Near Lublin, OK. Lublin. So, I went. And when I came there, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] that time Harry was there in the kibbutz. He was [HEBREW]. A [HEBREW] is like-- he was the buyer for food. And he worked also. And he knew that some-- some girl is supposed to come, beside there were girls and boys. So he left all of us out. And more or less, he found out what they-- when the-- the train will come. And he came fast, home. He left his job, and he came home. But when I came, I was very tired. So I asked the girls if I can I have a place to lay down. I said, I'm so worn out. I have to lay down and rest. So they told me here, here is bunk bed, one above the other on one side, the one above the other. This-- that was a girls' room. And they said here, on the first, go lay down and rest. Here I heard-- I heard a-- you now, a boy's voice, and says is she-- is she in? So they said, yes, she's in. But she's resting. You have time.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, the door was closed. But the door didn't have that lock. Not a handle, I don't know if somebody specially just left a hole. So he looks in through the hole. And as I was laying here, I-- I hear that. And I had a feeling. So I [? flattened ?] out. But he didn't know that I just turned myself just to the-- so he said that's the girl. That's the girl will be mine. The others shall not know. [LAUGHS] Well, three years took until we got married. And 56 years will be in June that we are married.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. And after the kibbutz, then you went on for more schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, after the kibbutz, I came home. And my-- my father was wasn't around anymore. And I started to help out my mother with-- with some business. It was like a children's business. And 1939, I went-- 1939, yeah-- I went to-- I-- I always wanted to be in medical field. So I went, and I found out that I can go to-- and sign up there as a school will be, you know, from September that year. But I have to give a test whether they will accept me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to Rowne. I took the test, and I passed it. And I became a student when the [INAUDIBLE] scholar. [NON-ENGLISH] is doctor's assistant, and [NON-ENGLISH] is [? a ?] mother. That was the school. Of course, we were-- we were taught everything by doctors, you know. And that was-- that was in Rowne. And how long were you in school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- I was until the day that the World War II broke out. And there's something else-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] You know, we always were thinking of each other, Harry-- Harry and I. And then after I hear that Harry told you that I went-- you know, we were corresponding by Poland, you know-- by the Russians, we could. And then I invited Harry he shall come to us. He probably didn't have a cover. But he said this time he would appreciate, on behalf of his mother and himself, I shall come. They invited me to there. So I went. [LAUGHS] This was already-- and he told you they had a six flat very beautiful building. But the Russians took it away. And they gave him a small little room, so I didn't have where to sleep. But his mother took me to her bed. [LAUGHS] I was sleeping with his mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did Harry meet your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't meet my mother. But my mother knew about Harry because I was always talking about Harry to my mother. And-- but because I got married in Rowne, being in school and my mother was in Horochow. But when the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got married while I was living with a few girls like in a dormitory. This was a private apartment and several girls were renting there, you know, the beds or whatever. Then Harry came. And he thought that let's get married. Right go here, go in, go here. So we got married. We rented a small little baby room. There was a cot and was a-- a baby bed in Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went to-- excuse me, we got married at a rabbi's apartment, the rabbi's home. We didn't have even a ring. Harry didn't have money to buy me a ring, and I didn't have it either. But-- but what did we buy? Where-- where I was living, there were two-- like a daughter with her husband and a-- and a daughter-in-law with her son. They were like two witnesses. They walked with us to the rabbi's house. And they bought a small bottle of whiskey and a pound of [NON-ENGLISH]. That was our wedding. And that was June 15, 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop here while we change tapes.
